title: Sorting - putting things in order, well or badly
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: Sorting arranges items in order. The naive way (compare every pair) is O(n^2); good general sorts (like the built-in one) are O(n log n).
explanation: There are many ways to sort, and they're a clean lesson in why Big-O matters. The obvious approach - walk the list comparing neighbours, again and again - is a loop inside a loop, O(n^2): fine for ten items, painful for a million. Smarter sorts split the work in half repeatedly (merge sort) or partition around a pivot (quicksort) and land at O(n log n). You'll usually just call the built-in sort, which is one of the good ones - but knowing the difference is knowing when 'it's slow' has a cause.

exercises.question: What does this print?
hints: sort() arranges in ascending order, in place.

exercises.question: A good general-purpose sort runs in...
hints: Better than the naive pair-by-pair way, not quite linear.
Merge sort and quicksort land here.

hints: n outer steps, each doing n inner comparisons.
